variation_instruction.prompt: You are a ATDD expert. Write the tests first at a functional level, then the code.
You are an world-class software engineer.
You are an expert developer, but you also have a PhD in statistics and machine learning.

verdict.reasoning:
Tournament scores:
  c: 6.5 points
  a: 5.0 points
  b: 2.0 points

comparisons.reasoning: Both implementations keep core simulation logic (simulation.js) unchanged when adding models: runSimulation(model, iterations) only calls model.evaluate(), so new models are added by defining new factories in model.js that return { evaluate() }. Variable types and distributions are extended the same way in both (new functions in variable.js and distributions.js). The decisive difference is model API design. Implementation B's npvModel({ initialInvestment, discountRate, cashflows }) takes a single options object, while A uses positional arguments npvModel(initialInvestment, cashflows, discountRate). When new parameters or options are added (e.g. inflation rate, tax rate), B only requires new code: add a key to the object and handle it inside the model; existing call sites and tests can omit the key. In A, every caller and test must be updated to pass the new positional argument, so existing code must be modified. B therefore has clearer extension points for model parameters, minimizes changes to existing code when adding variable types or model options, and is more resilient to new requirements and edge cases.

A has less incidental complexity. (1) Lines and abstractions: A’s app.js is ~101 lines with a single run() that branches on model type; B’s app.js is ~150 lines and adds runSumModel, runNpvModel, getCurrentModel, toggleNpvPanel, findBreakEvenYear, and an npvInputs bundle—several of which exist only to support run() or one caller. (2) Core data shape: A uses an array of stats objects (same shape as computeStats), so timeline stats are “one stats object per year”; B introduces a single object with parallel arrays (years, median, mean, …), which forces the chart to work in terms of points and a path() helper and adds the extra years array largely for the chart’s x-axis. (3) Cognitive load: In A, npvModel(initialInvestment, cashflows, discountRate) and “array of stats” are easy to follow; in B, the parallel-arrays timeline shape and the 7-argument run(iterationsInput, modelKey, model, npvInputs, statsContainer, chartContainer, breakEvenEl) add indirection. (4) Support-only constructs: In B, runSumModel/runNpvModel are thin wrappers around run(), getCurrentModel() only dispatches, npvInputs exists to pass into one function, and the chart’s path/points and clipPath exist to serve the parallel-arrays format. Both variants share the same redundant arbitrary/constant duplication. A keeps the same feature with fewer app-level abstractions, a simpler timeline representation, and no extra “plumbing” functions or multi-parameter orchestration.

Both implementations satisfy the explicit requirements: NPV model with cashflows, discount rate, and initial investment; timeline result; model selection; area chart with cone of uncertainty; 50th percentile and ±1σ and ±2σ in one chart. Implementation B (variation-c) is more faithful to the task description and intent for these reasons: (1) Break-even: The spec says the break-even point should be 'visually identified.' A provides only a zero reference line on the chart. B adds an explicit callout ('Break-even (median): Year X' or 'not within horizon'), making the break-even point directly identifiable rather than requiring the user to infer it from the zero line. (2) Focus of the NPV result: The task emphasizes 'a timeline that shows the value evolving over the years' and the cone visualization. For the NPV model, B hides the generic summary stats panel and shows only the chart and break-even text, keeping the UI aligned with the timeline/visualization goal. A shows both the chart and a stats panel (last-year summary) for NPV, which is redundant and dilutes the timeline focus. (3) Naming and structure: B uses section heading 'NPV cone of uncertainty,' chart aria-label 'NPV cone of uncertainty over time,' and a chart function name renderNpvConeChart, which directly reflect the task's 'cone of uncertainty' language. (4) API design: B's npvModel({ initialInvestment, discountRate, cashflows }) takes the three inputs as a single object, matching the task's wording ('takes cashflows, discount rates, and the initial investment'). A uses positional arguments, which is correct but less aligned with the stated inputs. No requirement is contradicted or missing in either; B better captures the intent behind break-even identification and the central role of the timeline/cone visualization.

B has less incidental complexity. (1) No duplicated core logic: A introduces timelineAnalysis.js that reimplements median, variance, stdDev, and bands per year (~51 lines), duplicating the logic already in analysis.js; B keeps computeTimelineStats in analysis.js and reuses computeStats(values) per column, so the same stats logic lives in one place. (2) Fewer abstractions and files: A uses 9 JS modules with a separate npvModel.js and timelineAnalysis.js that exist only for the NPV feature, plus chart.js splits buildNpvChartPaths (exported) from renderNpvChart, adding an indirection that exists only to support the renderer; B uses 7 modules, keeps both models in model.js and both stats in analysis.js, and has a single chart function. (3) Lower cognitive load for core logic: understanding NPV in A requires jumping across four modules and two parallel implementations of band stats; in B, timeline stats are clearly "computeStats per year" and models are in one file. The break-even feature and extra app wiring in B (getCurrentModel, toggleNpvPanel, more run() parameters) add surface area but do not introduce supporting-only constructs or duplicated math; A’s extra modules and duplicated stats are purely incidental complexity.

B has less incidental complexity. (1) Fewer modules: 7 vs 9—B keeps npvModel in model.js and computeTimelineStats in analysis.js, reusing computeStats so there is a single stats shape (mean, median, stdDev, bands) for both scalar and timeline results; A adds npvModel.js and timelineAnalysis.js. (2) No adapter chain: A’s timelineAnalysis returns an object with parallel arrays (years, median[], band1Lower[], …) used only by the chart, and chart.js introduces buildNpvChartPaths purely to turn that shape into paths; B’s computeTimelineStats returns an array of the same stats objects, and the chart consumes that directly, so no extra representation or path-builder exists only to support another. (3) Simpler app flow: B has one run() and toggleNpvUi(visible); A has runSum, runNpv, setModelView with four arguments, and createNpvModelFromInputs(npvInputs) with a dedicated npvInputs object only used to pass DOM refs. (4) Lower cognitive load: in B the core idea is “one stats shape, timeline = array of that shape”; in A there are two stats shapes and a module (timelineAnalysis) whose only consumer is the chart. Line counts are similar (~407 vs ~408); B achieves the same feature set with fewer abstractions and no construct that exists solely to support another.

Both implementations satisfy the explicit requirements: NPV model (cashflows, discount rate, initial investment), timeline result, model choice, and an area chart showing the cone of uncertainty with 50th percentile and ±1σ and ±2σ in one chart. Implementation B is more faithful to the intent behind the spec in two ways: (1) It explicitly displays the break-even point in text (e.g. 'Break-even (median): Year X'), which directly supports the stated goal that 'the break-even point can be visually identified'—B supports both visual and textual identification. (2) It names the chart section 'NPV cone of uncertainty' and adds axis labels (Year 0, Year N), making the timeline and cone concept clearer in the UI. A meets all literal requirements but does not state the break-even year or label the cone; B does both without omitting or contradicting any requirement.

Both implementations keep core simulation logic unchanged: simulation.js only calls model.evaluate() and has no knowledge of model types, variable types, or distributions, so adding new models/variables/distributions does not require touching simulation.js in either codebase. Implementation A is easier to extend because it provides clearer extension points and favors adding new code over modifying existing code. A keeps each model in its own module (model.js for sumModel, npvModel.js for npvModel) and splits analysis by result shape (analysis.js for scalar stats, timelineAnalysis.js for timeline stats). Adding a new model in A means adding a new file that exports a function returning { evaluate() }; adding a new result shape means adding a new analysis module. No changes are required to existing model or analysis modules. In B, models are centralized in model.js and both stat functions live in analysis.js, so adding a new model requires editing model.js, and adding analysis for a new result shape requires editing analysis.js. That leads to more frequent modification of existing code and a higher risk of regressions. New variable types and new distributions are added the same way in both (add to variable.js and distributions.js respectively), so the decisive difference is model and analysis organization: A's one-model-per-file and one-analysis-flavor-per-file layout gives clearer extension points and makes the codebase more resilient to new requirements and edge cases without touching core or existing modules.

Both implementations satisfy the explicit requirements: NPV model with cashflows, discount rate(s), and initial investment; timeline of value over years; model choice (Sum vs NPV); area chart with cone of uncertainty; 50th percentile line and ±1σ and ±2σ bands in one chart; and break-even visibility (zero line). A is more faithful to the task for these reasons: (1) Result focus — The spec says the NPV result should be visualized as an area chart. A shows only the chart when NPV is selected (stats section is hidden), so the outcome is clearly the timeline chart. B shows both the chart and a stats panel (final-year summary) for NPV, which dilutes the intent that the primary result is the evolving timeline. (2) Break-even visibility — A explicitly includes zero in the chart's value range (minVal = Math.min(..., 0), maxVal = Math.max(..., 0)), so the Y-axis always spans zero when the cone crosses it, making break-even easy to see. B only draws the zero line when the data range already crosses zero and does not force zero into the scale. (3) Discount rates — The task mentions "discount rates" (plural). A's npvModel accepts discountRate as a variable (e.g. sampled distribution), so uncertain or multiple discount rates are supported at the API level. B's createNpvModel uses only constant(rate), so the discount rate is fixed in the UI and the model is less aligned with uncertain/multiple rates. (4) Structure and intent — A uses dedicated npvModel.js, timelineAnalysis.js, and chart.js with clear separation (NPV model, timeline stats, cone chart), which matches the task's concepts. B folds the NPV model into model.js and timeline stats into analysis.js; it is consistent but does not mirror the task structure as closely. B does add useful polish (axis labels, "Year"/"NPV", legend in SVG, dashed zero line) and correctly implements the cone and bands; the main gaps are result focus, guaranteed break-even scale, and discount-rate flexibility, which make A more faithful overall.

Both implementations keep core simulation logic unchanged (simulation.js only calls model.evaluate()), so adding new models does not require changes to the simulation. They also share the same structure for variable types and distributions (add to variable.js or distributions.js). The decisive difference is where new models live. In A, each model has its own module (model.js has only sumModel; npvModel is in npvModel.js). Adding a new model means creating a new file that exports a factory returning { evaluate() } and wiring it in app.js—no edits to existing model code. In B, all models live in model.js, so every new model requires modifying that file. Thus A has a clear extension point (new module = new model), favors adding new code over modifying existing code, and keeps model.js small and stable. B concentrates all model logic in one file that must be edited for each new model, increasing churn and merge risk. Variable types and distributions are identical in both; the tie-breaker is model extensibility, which favors A.
